comment_text.sn35.241:1.1: This appears to be a mistake, as Kosambī was in fact on the Yamunā river.
comment_text.sn35.241:7.1: Nanda is one of the most common names in Pali, and it is not possible to identify this Nanda with any confidence. This episode stands as a reminder that the Buddha’s teachings were open, and many people from different backgrounds may have been listening, quite apart from the “mendicants” to whom the discourse is typically addressed. Note too that a man of such humble livelihood has no qualms in speaking directly to the Buddha.

root_text.sn35.241:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kosambiyaṁ viharati gaṅgāya nadiyā tīre.
root_text.sn35.241:1.2: Addasā kho bhagavā mahantaṁ dārukkhandhaṁ gaṅgāya nadiyā sotena vuyhamānaṁ.
root_text.sn35.241:1.4: “passatha no tumhe, bhikkhave, amuṁ mahantaṁ dārukkhandhaṁ gaṅgāya nadiyā sotena vuyhamānan”ti?
root_text.sn35.241:1.6: “Sace so, bhikkhave, dārukkhandho na orimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchati, na pārimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchati, na majjhe saṁsīdissati, na thale ussīdissati, na manussaggāho gahessati, na amanussaggāho gahessati, na āvaṭṭaggāho gahessati, na antopūti bhavissati;
root_text.sn35.241:1.7: evañhi so, bhikkhave, dārukkhandho samuddaninno bhavissati samuddapoṇo samuddapabbhāro.
root_text.sn35.241:1.9: Samuddaninno, bhikkhave, gaṅgāya nadiyā soto samuddapoṇo samuddapabbhāro.
root_text.sn35.241:2.1: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, sace tumhepi na orimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchatha, na pārimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchatha;
root_text.sn35.241:2.2: na majjhe saṁsīdissatha, na thale ussīdissatha, na manussaggāho gahessati, na amanussaggāho gahessati, na āvaṭṭaggāho gahessati, na antopūtī bhavissatha;
root_text.sn35.241:2.3: evaṁ tumhe, bhikkhave, nibbānaninnā bhavissatha nibbānapoṇā nibbānapabbhārā.
root_text.sn35.241:2.6: Evaṁ vutte, aññataro bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.241:2.7: “kiṁ nu kho, bhante, orimaṁ tīraṁ, kiṁ pārimaṁ tīraṁ, ko majjhe saṁsādo, ko thale ussādo, ko manussaggāho, ko amanussaggāho, ko āvaṭṭaggāho, ko antopūtibhāvo”ti?
root_text.sn35.241:3.1: “‘Orimaṁ tīran’ti kho, bhikkhu, channetaṁ ajjhattikānaṁ āyatanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.241:3.2: ‘Pārimaṁ tīran’ti kho, bhikkhu, channetaṁ bāhirānaṁ āyatanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn35.241:4.2: Idha, bhikkhu, gihīhi saṁsaṭṭho viharati, sahanandī sahasokī, sukhitesu sukhito, dukkhitesu dukkhito, uppannesu kiccakaraṇīyesu attanā tesu yogaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn35.241:5.2: Idha, bhikkhu, ekacco aññataraṁ devanikāyaṁ paṇidhāya brahmacariyaṁ carati: ‘imināhaṁ sīlena vā vatena vā tapena vā brahmacariyena vā devo vā bhavissāmi devaññataro vā’ti.
root_text.sn35.241:6.2: Idha, bhikkhu, ekacco dussīlo hoti pāpadhammo asucisaṅkassarasamācāro paṭicchannakammanto assamaṇo samaṇapaṭiñño abrahmacārī brahmacāripaṭiñño antopūti avassuto kasambujāto.
root_text.sn35.241:7.1: Tena kho pana samayena nando gopālako bhagavato avidūre ṭhito hoti.
root_text.sn35.241:7.2: Atha kho nando gopālako bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.241:7.3: “ahaṁ kho, bhante, na orimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchāmi, na pārimaṁ tīraṁ upagacchāmi, na majjhe saṁsīdissāmi, na thale ussīdissāmi, na maṁ manussaggāho gahessati, na amanussaggāho gahessati, na āvaṭṭaggāho gahessati, na antopūti bhavissāmi.
root_text.sn35.241:7.4: Labheyyāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, labheyyaṁ upasampadan”ti.
root_text.sn35.241:7.5: “Tena hi tvaṁ, nanda, sāmikānaṁ gāvo niyyātehī”ti.
root_text.sn35.241:7.8: Atha kho nando gopālako sāmikānaṁ gāvo niyyātetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.241:7.10: Labheyyāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, labheyyaṁ upasampadan”ti.
root_text.sn35.241:7.11: Alattha kho nando gopālako bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, alattha upasampadaṁ.
root_text.sn35.241:7.12: Acirūpasampanno ca panāyasmā nando eko vūpakaṭṭho …pe…
root_text.sn35.241:7.13: aññataro ca panāyasmā nando arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.sn35.241:0.3: The Simile of the Tree Trunk (1st)
translation_text.sn35.241:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Kosambī on the bank of the Ganges river.
translation_text.sn35.241:1.2: Seeing a large tree trunk being carried along by the current,
translation_text.sn35.241:1.4: “Mendicants, do you see that large tree trunk being carried along by the current of the Ganges river?”
translation_text.sn35.241:1.6: “Mendicants, assume that that tree trunk doesn’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And assume that it doesn’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and that it doesn’t rot away.
translation_text.sn35.241:1.7: In that case, that tree trunk will slant, slope, and incline towards the ocean.
translation_text.sn35.241:1.9: Because the current of the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines towards the ocean.
translation_text.sn35.241:2.1: In the same way, assume that you don’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And assume that you don’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and that you don’t rot away.
translation_text.sn35.241:2.3: In that case, you will slant, slope, and incline towards extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.241:2.5: Because right view slants, slopes, and inclines towards extinguishment.”
translation_text.sn35.241:2.6: When he said this, one of the mendicants asked the Buddha:
translation_text.sn35.241:2.7: “But sir, what’s the near shore and what’s the far shore? What’s sinking in the middle? What’s getting stranded on high ground? What’s getting taken by humans or non-humans? What’s getting caught up in a whirlpool? And what’s rotting away?”
translation_text.sn35.241:3.1: “‘The near shore’, mendicant, is a term for the six interior sense fields.
translation_text.sn35.241:3.2: ‘The far shore’ is a term for the six exterior sense fields.
translation_text.sn35.241:3.3: ‘Sinking in the middle’ is a term for greed and relishing.
translation_text.sn35.241:3.4: ‘Stranded on high ground’ is a term for the conceit ‘I am’.
translation_text.sn35.241:4.1: And what’s getting taken by humans?
translation_text.sn35.241:4.2: It’s when someone mixes closely with laypeople, sharing their joys and sorrows—happy when they’re happy and sad when they’re sad—and getting themselves involved in their business.
translation_text.sn35.241:4.3: That’s called getting taken by humans.
translation_text.sn35.241:5.1: And what’s getting taken by non-humans?
translation_text.sn35.241:5.2: It’s when someone leads the spiritual life wishing to be reborn in one of the orders of gods: ‘By this precept or observance or fervent austerity or spiritual practice, may I become one of the gods!’
translation_text.sn35.241:5.3: That’s called getting taken by non-humans.
translation_text.sn35.241:5.4: ‘Caught up in a whirlpool’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.sn35.241:6.2: It’s when some person is unethical, of bad qualities, filthy, with suspicious behavior, underhand, no true ascetic or spiritual practitioner—though claiming to be one—rotten inside, festering, and depraved.
translation_text.sn35.241:7.1: Now at that time Nanda the cowherd was sitting not far from the Buddha.
translation_text.sn35.241:7.2: Then he said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn35.241:7.3: “I won’t drift towards the near shore or the far shore, or sink in the middle, or get stranded on high ground. And I won’t get taken by humans or non-humans or caught up in a whirlpool, and I won’t rot away.
translation_text.sn35.241:7.4: Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?”
translation_text.sn35.241:7.5: “Well then, Nanda, return the cows to their owners.”
translation_text.sn35.241:7.6: “Sir, the cows will go back by themselves, since they love their calves.”
translation_text.sn35.241:7.7: “Still, Nanda, you should return them to their owners.”
translation_text.sn35.241:7.8: Then Nanda, after returning the cows to their owners, went up to the Buddha and said to him,
translation_text.sn35.241:7.9: “Sir, I have returned the cows to their owners.
translation_text.sn35.241:7.10: May I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?”
translation_text.sn35.241:7.11: And the cowherd Nanda received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence.
translation_text.sn35.241:7.13: Venerable Nanda became one of the perfected.

variant_text.sn35.241:2.7: saṁsādo → saṁsīdo (bj, pts1ed, mr); saṁsīdito (sya-all, km)
variant_text.sn35.241:7.9: niyyātitā → niyyāditā (bj, pts1ed); niyyātā (bj, sya-all, km)
